speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Israel. Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to-- with Youth Aliyah to a school where I lived. It was agricultural school called [INAUDIBLE]. And there, we studied half a day and half a day we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like a kibbutz? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not a kibbutz. It was-- it was a school from Youth Aliyah where they brought in all these children from-- from Europe that had no parents and no home to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet here any other children that went through similar experiences that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I want to tell you, as soon as I came to Israel, as soon as I came to this Youth Aliyah school, everybody asked me where are you from? Where are you born? I said, I'm a Sabra. I did not want to talk about it. I didn't want to admit that I was there, and I didn't want to be part of the people that this happened to. And I admired the Sabras, the Israeli-born young people, and I admired the Israeli people in general because they were so brave, because they had an army, because they had guns, because they had planes. And I could not understand, or did not want to be part of people that I was part of. That-- that this happened to. And that nobody helped us to defend ourselves. So not to have to cope with it, I totally denied it, and didn't want to-- to be at all, at all associated with people that went through this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ashamed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was ashamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was ashamed to be part of a society that could allow something like that to happen. I was ashamed of humanity for allowing men to do such terrible thing to men. And I did not want to-- to believe that there was such a humanity, that there was such a world, so I didn't want to-- to say that it was. And I felt if I said that it wasn't, then maybe it wasn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So for years and years and years, I did not ever say that I'm a Holocaust survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I went to this school called [INAUDIBLE] where I got part of my elementary school education, the latter part. Then, I went to a school called Mikveh Israel, which was my high school education. This was a high school, also a agricultural Youth Aliyah school. And after that, I went to the army where I served for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Israel did you know how to read or write?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew how to read French and I knew how to read Czech and write Czech. I knew how to read Hebrew because of the prayers that my uncle taught us in Czechoslovakia, how to pray. So that is Hebrew. So that's how I knew that. And also because in Belgium, I went to something called Sunday school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came out of the Israeli army what were your plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no plans. It was-- it was a cultural shock, because it was for the first time in my life that I was by myself. And I said to myself, what now? Where do I go from here? The army is wonderful, but you-- you, you know, don't study a profession.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- when I was in the army, I was the secretary to the director of the hospital, but I was more of the public relations person rather than a secretary, because I didn't know how to type. I didn't know how to take steno. So I came out of the Army ready to be a public relations person without education, you know, for-- for such a profession. So I got myself different jobs and my uncle, the one who lived in Belgium, since then moved to the United States, gave me a present-- a ticket to the United States and money for a college education. So I came to America, and I went to college. Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, Yeshiva University, Stern College for Women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and then I met my first husband, and I had two beautiful daughters. One is Miriam and one is Michelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your first husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first husband I met in the summer of my-- my college. All the students went to work in the Catskill Mountains of New York state in hotels as counselors or as waiters. So I came to work there as a counselor and my husband was there working also. And that's how we met. And then, 23 years later, we got divorced, and I moved back to Israel. And two years after I lived in Israel, I met Barney. He came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait for Barney for a minute. Your daughters, they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born in the United States, and they went to school in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughters came, and they studied at Bar-Ilan University in Israel. They went with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you come back here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back in '85, 1985.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met your present husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came back to the United States did your children come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they stayed because they since married. And-- they-- they came for a while. My oldest daughter lived in Houston, Texas, where she studied and received her master's degree in physical education, no physical therapist. She's a physical therapist. Miriam--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam. And my other daughter, Michelle, she married and she lived in Los Angeles. And then, in 1983, I believe, they both moved back to Israel. And now they both live in Israel. They're married. Miriam is married to a wonderful young man called Mark, Mark Garson. Michelle is married to a wonderful young man called Ron Katz. And Miriam has three children. One is that Daniella, one is Ariela, and one is Meira. And Michelle has three wonderful children. Arie, named after my father, Mindy, and Yitzchak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you spend time with your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I go to Israel twice a year, and I stay six weeks each time I go.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your daughters were growing up, did they know your background?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew that I was a Holocaust survivor and that I couldn't talk about it. That I just-- I was too emotional. I couldn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, would you ever tell us? And I said, yes. One day I'll tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your first husband, [? when ?] you married, how did you practice Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox. Modern Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kosher home. We didn't-- I travel on Saturday. We kept all the holidays. We went to synagogue. We didn't watch TV or listen to radio on Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're modern Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you practice Judaism today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, it's-- it's very interesting because Barney, my present husband, is not religious at all. But he lets me be what I am, and I don't interfere with what he does. So on Shabbat I don't travel, and I don't put on lights, and I still do my thing. And if he has to go somewhere, and he wants to go, he goes in his car and goes. But he practices a wonderful-- the wonderful way of Judaism. He's a great philanthropist, and he helps-- he helps Israel. He helps America. He just is a great humanitarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your first husband do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd rather not talk about my first husband, if you don't mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what does Barney do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barney's a semi-retired businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as an evening manager at a King David Hotel in Jerusalem, and he came on a AIPAC mission, which is American Israel Public Affairs Committee. And he-- he was an officer of AIPAC. And I was talking to friends of mine from New York, Louise and Mike Stein, who were AIPAC officers, also. And they were also Barney's friends, and they were my friends. And they were talking to me and Barney passed by, and he said, could you introduce me to this young lady? And they said yes, and they introduced us. And then I said, excuse me. I'm in the middle of my work and I have to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Congressman-- Congressman Tom Lantos and Annette Lantos were also in Israel at the time, and they were also at the King David. And Barney, being their best friend, asked them to-- to try to be intermediaries somehow. So they came over to me, and they said, excuse us. We'd like to talk to you. Could you meet us for some drinks in the lounge? So I said, well, I'm working now, but I'm through at midnight. I'll be happy to. And so I met them at-- in the lounge, and Barney was there with them. And we were talking and about five minutes later, Congressman Lantos very seriously, as only he can be when he wants to point-- prove a point, and Annette, too, because she was in on it, and he said, oh my goodness. I have a very important meeting to go to. And mind you, this was midnight. And so Annette said, oh yes, yes. We have to go. So they went and they left the two of us alone and-- Did you know who Tom Lantos was then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew he was a famous United States congressman, yes. And I know that his wife Annette was also very, very active in all the right things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you get married in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we got married in the United States. We got married in Alaska.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back-- why-- why did you come back? Did you come back to get married, or did you come back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well because--

speaker: interviewer
text: --for other reasons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barney and I dated, and he came back many times to date and to-- to meet and to get to know each other. And then he proposed, so we both decided to try if I could live in Alaska. So I came to Alaska, and I stayed there for one year to see if it worked, and it did. So we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you met him did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in '85 and we got married in '86, one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still live in Alaska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I live in Alaska part time, and I live part time in Hawaii, and I live three months in Israel. We have an apartment in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a chance to tell the children of the future anything that's very important for you, what would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say be very tolerant, because tolerance will not bring about hate of one religion to another, or one race, or one color. Tolerance can only bring love, and if there's love and tolerance, then there cannot be any such terrible atrocities that we saw then. And I would tell them just to try to have hope. No matter what happens and no matter what terrible situation you are, don't give in and don't despair. Have hope, because at the end of every horrible, black tunnel, there's a beautiful rainbow.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to thank you. I want to thank your camera lady and camera men for making it possible so that future generations can know what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Rachel, would you introduce your husband to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. This is my husband, Barnard J. Gottstein, who we call short Barney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Short Barney? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been married 10 years. We have-- we got married July 2, 1986.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Anchorage, Alaska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you live where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We live in Anchorage, Alaska, and in Maui, Hawaii, and three months out of the year in [INAUDIBLE] in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Gottstein, when you married Rachel, did you know that she was a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, sure. In fact, we went together to Yad Vashem for the first time she had seen Yad Vashem. Was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who instigated it?

speaker: interviewee
text: AIPAC. AIPAC had a tour to Yad Vashem, and I said I don't want to go. And my husband said, if I go would you like to try?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have five children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they live where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four of them live in Alaska, and one in Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to tell us about Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are many things I could tell about Rachel. She's a very, very amazing person, that after all of the things she went through that she came out with the gentleness and the kindness and spirit that she has, which would be unusual to somebody that hadn't gone through what she went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, how about you? Is there anything you would like to tell Barney at the present time?

speaker: interviewee
text: To say about Barney? To just tell you about Barney? Well, I think after you go through an experience like I went through, every day of your life that your live should be very meaningful, and should be with a very meaningful person. And Barney is a very, very special person in that that he does so much for humanity. He does it for the Christian world, the Jewish world, he even, I mean not even, but he wants to do for the Muslim world. He just wants to help humanity. And for me, this is regaining my faith in humanity when I see how much a person does. So I'm just totally in awe of all the things that Barney does. And I'm so happy and lucky that I met such a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just meant to ask you, how is your relationship with God?

speaker: interviewee
text: My relationship with God is very good. I hope his relationship with me is as good as mine with him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.
